questions.question: A 0.10 mol/L solution of a strong acid has a pH of:

choices.explanation: For a strong acid that completely dissociates: [H⁺] = 0.10 mol/L = 1.0 × 10⁻¹ mol/L; pH = −log(1.0 × 10⁻¹) = 1.
pH = 2 corresponds to [H⁺] = 0.010 mol/L; a 0.10 mol/L strong acid gives [H⁺] = 0.10 mol/L and pH = 1.
pH = 7 is neutral; a strong acid solution is acidic with pH < 7.
pH = 13 corresponds to pOH = 1, [OH⁻] = 0.10 mol/L; this is a 0.10 mol/L strong base, not acid.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly defines a Brønsted-Lowry base?

choices.text: A species that produces $\text{OH}^-$ ions in water
choices.explanation: This is the Arrhenius definition of a base, which is more limited; Brønsted-Lowry theory extends beyond species that produce OH⁻ in water.

choices.text: A species that accepts a proton ($\text{H}^+$) from an acid
choices.explanation: The Brønsted-Lowry definition of a base is a proton acceptor; it receives H⁺ from the acid in a reaction.

choices.text: A species that donates an electron pair to an acid
choices.explanation: This is the Lewis base definition, which is even broader than Brønsted-Lowry; Brønsted-Lowry specifically refers to proton transfer.

choices.text: A species with more OH⁻ ions than H⁺ ions at equilibrium
choices.explanation: This describes a basic solution, not the definition of a Brønsted-Lowry base (which can be any proton acceptor, even in non-aqueous systems).

questions.question: The $K_a$ of acetic acid ($\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$) is $1.8 \times 10^{-5}$. Which of the following correctly calculates the pH of a 0.10 mol/L acetic acid solution?

choices.explanation: pH = 1.0 would require [H⁺] = 0.10 mol/L, which would be the case for a strong acid; acetic acid is weak and only partially dissociates, giving a higher pH.
4.74 = pKa of acetic acid; pH = pKa only at the half-equivalence point in a titration when [HA] = [A⁻], not for a 0.10 mol/L weak acid solution.
pH ≈ 5.0 would require [H⁺] = 10⁻⁵ mol/L; for 0.10 mol/L acetic acid, the approximation gives [H⁺] ≈ 1.34 × 10⁻³ mol/L → pH ≈ 2.87.

questions.question: At 25°C, $K_w = 1.0 \times 10^{-14}$. In a basic solution where $[\text{OH}^-] = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ mol/L, what is the pH?

choices.explanation: pH = 3 would mean [H⁺] = 10⁻³ mol/L, which is acidic; the given solution is basic (pOH = 3, so pH = 11).
pH = 7 indicates a neutral solution; [OH⁻] = 10⁻³ mol/L is significantly greater than 10⁻⁷ mol/L, making the solution basic.
pH = 14 would correspond to [H⁺] = 10⁻¹⁴ mol/L and [OH⁻] = 1.0 mol/L; the given [OH⁻] = 10⁻³ mol/L gives pH = 11.

questions.question: Based on the table, which of the following correctly ranks these acids from strongest to weakest?

choices.text: HCN > $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$ > $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ > HF
choices.explanation: Acid strength increases with K_a; HCN has the smallest K_a and is the weakest acid, not the strongest.

choices.text: HF > $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ > $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$ > HCN
choices.explanation: Larger K_a indicates stronger acid. In order: K_a(HF) = 7.2×10⁻⁴ > K_a(CH₃COOH) = 1.8×10⁻⁵ > K_a(H₂CO₃) = 4.3×10⁻⁷ > K_a(HCN) = 6.2×10⁻¹⁰.

choices.text: $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ > HF > HCN > $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$
choices.explanation: HF has K_a = 7.2×10⁻⁴, which is larger than K_a(CH₃COOH) = 1.8×10⁻⁵; HF is stronger than acetic acid.

choices.text: HF > HCN > $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$ > $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$
choices.explanation: This ordering incorrectly places HCN (K_a = 6.2×10⁻¹⁰) above H₂CO₃ (K_a = 4.3×10⁻⁷); H₂CO₃ is stronger than HCN.

questions.question: A buffer is most effective at resisting pH changes when:

choices.text: The pH is far from the $pK_a$ of the weak acid
choices.explanation: Buffers are most effective near pH = pKa; at pH values far from pKa, one component of the buffer pair is nearly exhausted.

choices.text: The ratio of $[\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}]$ is close to 1 (i.e., pH ≈ $pK_a$)
choices.explanation: The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation shows that when [A⁻]/[HA] ≈ 1, both the acid and conjugate base are present in roughly equal amounts, providing maximum buffering capacity.

choices.text: The buffer contains a strong acid and its conjugate base
choices.explanation: Strong acids fully dissociate and have no appreciable conjugate base equilibrium; buffers require a weak acid (or weak base) component.

choices.text: The total buffer concentration is very low, spreading the acid and base components thinly
choices.explanation: Higher buffer concentration provides greater capacity to absorb acid or base; very dilute buffers have low capacity and are less effective.

stimulus.content:
The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation is: $\text{pH} = pK_a + \log\dfrac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$
A buffer is prepared using acetic acid ($pK_a = 4.74$) with $[\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}] = 0.20$ mol/L and $[\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-] = 0.40$ mol/L.

questions.question: What is the pH of this buffer?

choices.explanation: 4.44 = 4.74 + log(0.5) = 4.74 − 0.30 = 4.44; this would apply if [A⁻]/[HA] = 0.5 (i.e., [A⁻] < [HA]). The given ratio is 0.40/0.20 = 2.0 (A⁻ > HA), giving pH > pKa.
4.74 = pKa, which applies when [A⁻] = [HA]; the given solution has [A⁻] = 2[HA], so pH > pKa.
3.44 is far too low for an acetic acid buffer; it would require [H⁺] ≈ 0.0036 mol/L, which is not consistent with a buffered solution near pH 5.

questions.question: In the autoionization of water, $\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq)$, $K_w = [\text{H}^+][\text{OH}^-] = 1.0 \times 10^{-14}$ at 25°C. Which of the following correctly describes a neutral solution?

choices.explanation: This describes an acidic solution, not a neutral one.

choices.text: $[\text{H}^+] = [\text{OH}^-] = 1.0 \times 10^{-7}$ mol/L; pH = 7
choices.explanation: In a neutral solution, [H⁺] = [OH⁻]. With K_w = [H⁺][OH⁻] = 10⁻¹⁴: [H⁺] = [OH⁻] = √(10⁻¹⁴) = 10⁻⁷ mol/L; pH = 7.
This describes a basic solution where [OH⁻] > [H⁺]; a neutral solution requires equal concentrations.

choices.text: $[\text{H}^+] = 0$; the solution contains no protons
choices.explanation: Water always autoionizes to produce a small but non-zero [H⁺] = 10⁻⁷ mol/L; complete absence of H⁺ is not physically possible in an aqueous solution.

title.text: Titration Curve: 0.10 mol/L CH₃COOH with 0.10 mol/L NaOH

questions.question: Based on the titration curve, which of the following correctly identifies the equivalence point and the half-equivalence point?

choices.text: Equivalence point: 25 mL (pH = 4.74); half-equivalence point: 50 mL (pH = 7.00)
choices.explanation: The equivalence point is where all acid is neutralized; pH = 4.74 at 25 mL is the half-equivalence point (pH = pKa). The equivalence point occurs at 50 mL (pH ≈ 8.7, not 7).

choices.text: Equivalence point: 50 mL (pH > 7); half-equivalence point: 25 mL (pH = 4.74 = $pK_a$)
choices.explanation: The equivalence point is where equal moles of acid and base react (50 mL); pH > 7 because the salt (sodium acetate) is basic due to hydrolysis. At 25 mL (half-equivalence), [HA] = [A⁻] and pH = pKa = 4.74.

choices.text: Equivalence point: 40 mL (pH = 7.00); half-equivalence point: 20 mL (pH = 4.74)
choices.explanation: pH = 7 at the equivalence point occurs only for strong acid/strong base titrations; for a weak acid, the equivalence point pH is above 7.

choices.text: Equivalence point: 0 mL (pH = 2.87); half-equivalence point: 25 mL (pH = 4.74)
choices.explanation: 0 mL is the starting point (pure acid, not the equivalence point); the equivalence point is at 50 mL.

questions.question: The $K_a$ of acetic acid is $1.8 \times 10^{-5}$. What is the $K_b$ for the acetate ion ($\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-$)?

choices.explanation: Ka and Kb of a conjugate pair are not equal unless Kw = 1; they are related by Ka × Kb = Kw.
1.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ is Kw; Kb is calculated as Kw/Ka = 5.6 × 10⁻¹⁰.
1.8 × 10⁹ would result from Kb = Ka/Kw (inverting the correct relationship); the correct formula is Kb = Kw/Ka.

questions.question: Which of the following is the strongest acid?

choices.explanation: H₃PO₄ is a weak acid with Ka = 7.5 × 10⁻³, much smaller than HClO₄ which is a strong acid.
HF is a weak acid (Ka = 7.2 × 10⁻⁴); despite common misconceptions, HF is not a strong acid.

choices.text: $\text{HClO}_4$ (perchloric acid)
choices.explanation: HClO₄ is one of the strongest known acids — a strong acid that fully dissociates in water. Its Ka >> 1, far exceeding the values for the other choices.

choices.text: $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ ($K_a = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$)
choices.explanation: Acetic acid is a weak acid with Ka = 1.8 × 10⁻⁵; it is a much weaker acid than HClO₄.

stimulus.content: A student prepares an acetic acid/acetate buffer. She adds 0.010 mol of NaOH (strong base) to 1.0 L of a buffer containing 0.10 mol of acetic acid and 0.10 mol of sodium acetate.

questions.question: What happens to the pH of the buffer after the addition, and why?

choices.text: pH increases dramatically because the strong base overwhelms the buffer
choices.explanation: The buffer neutralizes the added base; 0.010 mol NaOH converts 0.010 mol HA to A⁻, leaving [HA] = 0.090 and [A⁻] = 0.11 mol — well within the buffer range.

choices.text: pH increases slightly; the NaOH converts some HA to A⁻, shifting the ratio $[\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}]$ upward
pH decreases slightly because the addition of NaOH increases the ionic strength
choices.explanation: Adding base increases pH, not decreases it; and ionic strength effects are secondary to the buffer equilibrium.

choices.text: pH remains exactly constant because the buffer has infinite capacity
choices.explanation: No buffer has infinite capacity; adding base does shift the [A⁻]/[HA] ratio, causing a small pH increase. The change is small but not zero.

questions.question: A solution with $[\text{OH}^-] = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/L at 25°C has a pH of:

choices.explanation: 4.60 is neither pOH nor pH for this solution; pOH = 3.60 and pH = 10.40.

stimulus.content: Phenolphthalein indicator changes color from colorless (acid form, HIn) to pink (base form, In⁻) in the pH range 8.2–10.0. Its $pK_a = 9.1$.

questions.question: At pH = 9.1, which of the following best describes the indicator?

choices.text: Colorless, because pH < 10.0
choices.explanation: At pH = pKa = 9.1, [HIn] = [In⁻]; the solution is mid-transition (pale pink), not colorless, because half the indicator is in the colored In⁻ form.

choices.text: The indicator is mid-transition with roughly equal amounts of HIn and In⁻, appearing faint pink
choices.explanation: At pH = pKa, the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation gives log([In⁻]/[HIn]) = 0, so [In⁻] = [HIn]; the solution appears pale pink (intermediate between colorless and full pink).

choices.text: Fully pink, because pH = 9.1 is within the color change range of 8.2–10.0
choices.explanation: Full pink color requires pH >> pKa (essentially all in the In⁻ form); at pH = pKa, only half is converted to In⁻.

choices.text: Colorless, because pKa > 7 indicates the indicator is a weak acid that stays in the HIn form
choices.explanation: The pKa value does not determine whether HIn predominates at pH = pKa; at pH = pKa specifically, [HIn] = [In⁻] (equal amounts of both forms).

questions.question: Which of the following aqueous salt solutions is basic at 25°C?

choices.text: $\text{NaCl}$
choices.explanation: NaCl is the salt of a strong acid (HCl) and strong base (NaOH); neither Na⁺ nor Cl⁻ hydrolyzes significantly, giving a neutral solution (pH = 7).

choices.text: $\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$
choices.explanation: NH₄Cl is the salt of a weak base (NH₃) and strong acid (HCl); NH₄⁺ hydrolyzes to give H⁺, making the solution acidic (pH < 7).

choices.text: $\text{CH}_3\text{COONa}$
choices.explanation: Sodium acetate is the salt of a weak acid (CH₃COOH) and strong base (NaOH); the acetate ion hydrolyzes water to produce OH⁻, making the solution basic (pH > 7).

choices.text: $\text{KNO}_3$
choices.explanation: KNO₃ is the salt of a strong acid (HNO₃) and strong base (KOH); neither K⁺ nor NO₃⁻ hydrolyzes, giving a neutral solution.

questions.question: Using the data in the table, what is the $K_b$ for the fluoride ion?

choices.explanation: 7.2 × 10⁻⁴ is Ka of HF, not Kb of F⁻; they are related by Ka × Kb = Kw.
7.2 × 10⁻¹⁸ would result from Ka × Kw rather than Kw/Ka; the correct formula is Kb = Kw/Ka.
1.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ is Kw, not Kb(F⁻); Kb is calculated as Kw/Ka(HF) = 1.4 × 10⁻¹¹.

questions.question: Which of the following species is amphoteric (can act as both an acid and a base)?

choices.text: $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$
choices.explanation: H₂SO₄ is a diprotic strong acid; it can only donate protons (act as an acid), not accept them.

choices.text: $\text{Na}^+$
choices.explanation: Na⁺ is a spectator ion from a strong base; it neither donates nor accepts protons in water and is neither acidic nor basic.

choices.text: $\text{HCO}_3^-$
choices.explanation: HCO₃⁻ can donate a proton (acting as acid: HCO₃⁻ → H⁺ + CO₃²⁻) or accept a proton (acting as base: HCO₃⁻ + H⁺ → H₂CO₃). It is amphoteric.

choices.text: $\text{Cl}^-$
choices.explanation: Cl⁻ is the conjugate base of a strong acid (HCl); it has essentially no tendency to accept a proton and acts only as a spectator ion in water.

stimulus.content: A student performs an experiment in which 25.0 mL of 0.100 mol/L weak acid HA ($pK_a = 5.00$) is titrated with 0.100 mol/L NaOH. The student records pH at various volumes of NaOH.

questions.question: At what volume of NaOH added does the pH equal $pK_a$, and what is the name of this point?

choices.text: 12.5 mL; the equivalence point
choices.explanation: At 12.5 mL (half-equivalence point), [HA] = [A⁻] and pH = pKa = 5.00; this is the half-equivalence point, not the equivalence point.

choices.text: 12.5 mL; the half-equivalence point
choices.explanation: At 12.5 mL, half the acid (0.00125 mol) has been neutralized; [HA] = [A⁻]. By Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = pKa + log(1) = pKa. This is the half-equivalence point.

choices.text: 25.0 mL; the equivalence point
choices.explanation: At 25.0 mL, all the acid is neutralized (equivalence point); pH > 7 (the conjugate base A⁻ hydrolyzes water). pH = pKa occurs at 12.5 mL (half-equivalence point).

choices.text: 0 mL; the initial point
choices.explanation: At 0 mL NaOH, only HA is present; pH < pKa for a weak acid with [HA] = 0.1 mol/L. pH = pKa only when half the acid has been neutralized.

questions.question: The percent dissociation of a weak acid decreases when the concentration of the acid is increased. Which of the following best explains this trend?

choices.text: Increasing concentration provides more molecules, but Ka is fixed; the fraction that can dissociate decreases (common-ion-like dilution effect)
choices.explanation: For HA ⇌ H⁺ + A⁻: percent dissociation = [H⁺]/[HA]₀ × 100%. As [HA]₀ increases, [H⁺] increases but less than proportionally (because Ka = [H⁺]²/[HA] is fixed). Thus x/C₀ decreases as C₀ increases.

choices.text: Higher acid concentration increases the rate of the reverse reaction, suppressing dissociation
choices.explanation: While higher product concentration does suppress dissociation, the fundamental reason is that Ka is fixed; as C₀ increases, the fraction dissociated decreases even though the absolute amount of H⁺ increases.

choices.text: Ka decreases at higher concentrations due to interionic interactions
choices.explanation: Ka is a thermodynamic constant at fixed temperature; it does not change with concentration (activity coefficients cause small deviations but the fundamental trend is not due to Ka changing).

choices.text: Percent dissociation and concentration are unrelated quantities
choices.explanation: They are related; for a weak acid HA with Ka = x²/(C₀ − x) ≈ x²/C₀, percent dissociation = (x/C₀) × 100% ≈ √(Ka/C₀) × 100%, which clearly decreases as C₀ increases.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why $\text{NH}_3$ is a weak base rather than a strong base?

choices.text: $\text{NH}_3$ is a gas and does not ionize in water
choices.explanation: NH₃ does ionize in water: NH₃ + H₂O ⇌ NH₄⁺ + OH⁻; it is a weak base because this equilibrium lies far to the left.

choices.text: $\text{NH}_3$ does not contain an OH group, so it cannot produce OH⁻ ions
choices.explanation: NH₃ does produce OH⁻ by accepting H⁺ from water; Brønsted-Lowry bases do not require an OH group.

choices.text: The equilibrium $\text{NH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{NH}_4^+ + \text{OH}^-$ lies mostly to the left ($K_b = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$)
choices.explanation: Kb = 1.8 × 10⁻⁵ << 1; the equilibrium strongly favors NH₃ and H₂O over NH₄⁺ and OH⁻. Only a small fraction of NH₃ molecules accept protons at any given time.

choices.text: $\text{NH}_3$ reacts with acids to form salts, making it acidic overall
choices.explanation: Reacting with acids is exactly what a base does; NH₃ reacting with HCl to form NH₄Cl is a base-acid neutralization reaction, confirming NH₃ is a base.

questions.question: Based on the table, why is the pH at the equivalence point greater than 7 when a weak acid is titrated with a strong base?

choices.text: The strong base is always in slight excess at the equivalence point
choices.explanation: At the equivalence point, exactly stoichiometric amounts of acid and base have reacted; neither is in excess.

choices.text: The conjugate base of the weak acid (A⁻) hydrolyzes water to produce OH⁻, raising the pH above 7
choices.explanation: At the equivalence point, the solution contains A⁻ (the conjugate base). A⁻ + H₂O ⇌ HA + OH⁻; this hydrolysis produces OH⁻, giving pH > 7.

choices.text: The weak acid is not fully neutralized at the equivalence point, leaving excess acid that reacts with the base
choices.explanation: By definition, at the equivalence point all the acid has been neutralized; the question is about the pH of the resulting salt solution, not about incomplete neutralization.

choices.text: The pH is greater than 7 because the resulting solution contains Na⁺, which is a Lewis acid
choices.explanation: Na⁺ does not hydrolyze water appreciably; it is a spectator ion from a strong base and has essentially no effect on pH.

questions.question: When designing a buffer system to maintain pH ≈ 7.4 (blood pH), which weak acid from the following table would be the most appropriate choice?

choices.text: Oxalic acid ($pK_{a1} = 1.23$)
choices.explanation: A buffer is most effective when pH ≈ pKa; pKa = 1.23 is far from 7.4, making oxalic acid an inappropriate choice.

choices.text: Acetic acid ($pK_a = 4.74$)
choices.explanation: pKa = 4.74 is about 2.7 units from the target pH of 7.4; buffers work best within ±1 pH unit of pKa.

choices.text: Carbonic acid ($pK_{a2} = 6.35$ for $\text{HCO}_3^-$/$\text{CO}_3^{2-}$)
choices.explanation: pKa2 = 6.35 is about 1 pH unit below 7.4; closer but still not optimal. The HCO₃⁻/H₂CO₃ pair (pKa1 ≈ 6.1) is what blood uses, but this choice refers to pKa2.

choices.text: Dihydrogen phosphate ($\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4^-$/$\text{HPO}_4^{2-}$, $pK_{a2} = 7.20$)
choices.explanation: pKa2 of phosphoric acid = 7.20, which is within 0.2 pH units of the target pH of 7.4; this gives a buffer with excellent capacity at physiological pH. Phosphate buffers are widely used in biochemistry.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly explains why HI is a stronger acid than HF in aqueous solution?

choices.text: HI has a larger molecular size, so it is more polar than HF
choices.explanation: HF is actually more polar than HI; the greater electronegativity of F creates a larger dipole. Polarity favors HF, not HI, in terms of making the bond polar.

choices.text: The H–I bond is weaker and longer than the H–F bond, making it easier to break and release H⁺
choices.explanation: Despite F being more electronegative (which would favor ionization of HF), the H–I bond is much weaker (lower bond energy) than H–F. Bond strength is the dominant factor; the weaker H–I bond breaks more easily, releasing H⁺.

choices.text: Iodide is a better leaving group because it is more electronegative than fluoride
choices.explanation: Fluorine is far more electronegative than iodine; however, iodide is a better leaving group because of its large size and high polarizability, not electronegativity.

choices.text: HF forms a special stable compound with water that reduces its ability to donate protons
choices.explanation: While HF does hydrogen bond strongly with water and its fluoride ion forms FHF⁻ dimers, the primary reason HI is a stronger acid is the much weaker H–I bond energy.

stimulus.content:
A student measures the pH at three points during a titration of 25.0 mL of 0.100 mol/L weak base ($\text{NH}_3$, $K_b = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$) with 0.100 mol/L HCl:
- Before adding HCl: pH = 11.13
- After adding 12.5 mL HCl: pH = 9.26
- After adding 25.0 mL HCl: pH = 5.28

questions.question: Which of the following correctly explains the pH = 5.28 at the equivalence point?

choices.text: The equivalence point has pH < 7 because HCl is a strong acid, making the solution acidic
choices.explanation: At the equivalence point, no excess HCl remains; the acidity comes from hydrolysis of NH₄⁺, not from free HCl.

choices.text: At the equivalence point, all NH₃ has been converted to $\text{NH}_4^+$ (a weak acid); $\text{NH}_4^+$ donates a proton to water, producing an acidic solution
choices.explanation: NH₄⁺ + H₂O ⇌ NH₃ + H₃O⁺; Ka(NH₄⁺) = Kw/Kb = 5.6×10⁻¹⁰. The resulting acidic solution has pH = 5.28.

choices.text: The solution is acidic because water donates protons to Cl⁻ at the equivalence point
choices.explanation: Cl⁻ (conjugate base of strong acid HCl) has essentially no tendency to accept protons from water; the pH is determined by NH₄⁺ hydrolysis.

choices.text: pH = 5.28 indicates excess HCl was added beyond the equivalence point
choices.explanation: The problem states this pH is measured at the equivalence point (25.0 mL); the acidic pH results from NH₄⁺ hydrolysis, not excess acid.

questions.question: A student adds 100 mL of 0.10 mol/L NaOH to 100 mL of 0.10 mol/L HCl. Which of the following best predicts the pH of the resulting solution?

choices.text: pH = 1, because the strong acid dominates
choices.explanation: Equal moles of HCl (0.010 mol) and NaOH (0.010 mol) are mixed; they completely neutralize each other, leaving only NaCl and water.

choices.text: pH = 7, because equal moles of strong acid and strong base neutralize completely
choices.explanation: Moles HCl = (0.100 L)(0.10 mol/L) = 0.010 mol; moles NaOH = 0.010 mol. 1:1 neutralization gives NaCl (neutral salt) + H₂O → pH = 7.

choices.text: pH = 14, because NaOH is a strong base
choices.explanation: The NaOH is completely consumed by the HCl; no excess OH⁻ remains in solution.

choices.text: pH ≈ 6, because mixing two solutions dilutes both and slightly reduces pH
choices.explanation: Dilution alone would not make the solution acidic; the neutralization goes to completion regardless of dilution, giving pH = 7 for a strong acid/strong base mixture at stoichiometric equivalence.
